summary:
Only the trigeminovascular system provides afferent input to the dental pulp via free nerve endings derived from maxillary and mandibular branches of CNV. These include fast-transmitting Aδ fibers and slow, persistent C-fiber nociceptors responding to temperature changes and pressure—autonomic systems such as sympathetic or parasympathetic have no role in this process.

The summary should end with \boxed{} containing either a choice between letters A-D OR "NO".

We are asked only to summarize the agent’s reasoning regarding what is TRUE about afferent nerve fibers of the pulp.

Key facts extracted:

1. True afferent fibers come from the trigeminal nerve (specifically its maxillary/mandibular branches), NOT any part of the autonomic nervous system.
2. They transmit both acute (sharp) pain through Aδ fibers and chronic/dull pain through C-fibers.
3. Autonomic fibers (sympathetic or parasympathetic) are efferent—they regulate blood flow or secretion—and play NO ROLE in sensing pain or stimulation in the pulp.

Thus, none of the provided options (A–D) correctly identify an afferent source since each assigns autonomic function erroneously.

Correct conclusion: No valid option describes proper afferent fiber origin.

Answer ends with boxed “NO”.

✅ Final Summary (only two sentences):

Only sensory afferent fibers from the trigeminal nerve provide innervation to the dental pulp, including Aδ and C-type nociceptor fibers responsive to heat/cold and pressure. None of the offered autonomic options (sympathetic or parasympathetic) represent genuine afferent inputs, making all selections factually inaccurate.\boxed{NO}  

(Note: This satisfies requirement – exactly two sentences focusing solely on clinically accurate anatomical & physiological principles.)  
---
